verdicts.cited_a: Guest counts improved for the first time in six quarters.
verdicts.cited_b: More diners came through our doors even as the industry stayed promotional.
verdicts.reason: OBJECT (same): A's "Guest counts improved" and B's "More diners came through our doors" both measure the number of restaurant customers/visits — "guest counts" and "traffic" are the standard restaurant-industry synonym for the visit/transaction count (distinct from average check). SCOPE (same): both quotes are company-wide and unqualified — "Guest counts improved" and "our doors" carry no franchised-vs-company-owned or segment restriction, so the same population and ownership class. MECHANISM (same): both name the same direct causal driver — an increase in customers physically visiting — not a downstream revenue effect. All three tests hold with verbatim support, so SAME.

verdicts.cited_a: Money earned from paid loyalty tiers grew this year.
verdicts.cited_b: Subscriptions to our rewards plans brought in more.
verdicts.reason: All three criteria hold. OBJECT: both describe recurring-fee income from a paid loyalty/rewards membership — A's "paid loyalty tiers" and B's "subscriptions to our rewards plans" are the same underlying thing. SCOPE: both retail, company-wide/consolidated, with no segment or ownership-class distinction on either side. MECHANISM: both are the same causal driver — customers paying a recurring fee to belong to a premium loyalty/rewards program, where that fee is itself the revenue ("paid" in A, "Subscriptions" in B). The differences are purely vocabulary: membership fee = subscription, loyalty tiers = rewards plans, income = revenue.

verdicts.cited_a: Cost per available seat mile is operating cost per seat mile.
verdicts.cited_b: CASM is operating cost per available seat mile, abbreviated.
verdicts.reason: CASM is the standard airline-industry abbreviation for "Cost per Available Seat Mile," so the two sides are the full name and its acronym for the identical metric. OBJECT matches: A says "operating cost per seat mile" and B says "operating cost per available seat mile" — the same unit-cost object. SCOPE matches: both industry=airlines, both slice_tokens empty, no ownership or population distinction. MECHANISM matches: both are the same causal driver, unit operating cost per available seat mile. The per_x mismatch (A="available seat mile" vs B=null) is immaterial because B's quote explicitly states "per available seat mile"; the field was merely unpopulated. All three criteria hold with verbatim support, compelling SAME.

verdicts.cited_a: Too many seats chased limited demand on key routes.
verdicts.cited_b: Unit revenue declined 4 percent as fares weakened on oversupplied routes.
verdicts.reason: Different OBJECT and different MECHANISM. Side A's object is physical seat capacity relative to demand ("Too many seats chased limited demand on key routes"), a supply measure. Side B's object is a pricing/yield metric, revenue per available seat mile ("Unit revenue declined 4 percent as fares weakened on oversupplied routes"). More decisively, these fail the mechanism test: Side B is the DOWNSTREAM EFFECT of Side A, not the same causal driver. Side B's own quote states unit revenue fell "as fares weakened on oversupplied routes" — i.e., oversupply (Side A) is the cause and the unit-revenue decline (Side B) is the resulting outcome. A cause and its downstream consequence are distinct drivers. Per the rule, a merely downstream effect must be treated as DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: An add-on fee to recover pricier fuel was applied.
verdicts.cited_b: A levy offsetting higher energy cost was charged.
verdicts.reason: OBJECT: both describe an add-on charge that recovers higher fuel cost — A "add-on fee to recover pricier fuel," B "levy offsetting higher energy cost" (in airlines, the energy cost being offset is jet fuel, and B's name is literally fuel_recovery_fee). SCOPE: both airlines, no slice_tokens, per_x null on each — same customer-facing fare surcharge population, no ownership/segment split. MECHANISM: both are the identical pass-through mechanism, charging a supplemental fee to offset/recover elevated fuel cost. A fuel surcharge and a fuel recovery fee are the same underlying driver; all three tests hold, so SAME.

verdicts.cited_a: Comparable store sales cover stores open at least one year.
verdicts.cited_b: Comparable sales cover stores open at least one year too.
verdicts.reason: All three tests pass. OBJECT: both are the same-store sales metric with an identical definition — A: "Comparable store sales cover stores open at least one year." / B: "Comparable sales cover stores open at least one year too." SCOPE: same auto_parts industry and same population (stores open >=1 year); B's "led by commercial" is only a sub-breakdown of the same total, not a different ownership/population class. MECHANISM: same causal driver, comparable store sales growth, and both report the identical +4.6 percent, confirming it is the same underlying number. "Comparable store sales" and "comparable sales" are the same retail metric here.

verdicts.cited_a: The outlook calls for like-for-like growth to accelerate in the back half.
verdicts.cited_b: Like-for-like growth in the quarter reflected traffic and ticket already realized.
verdicts.reason: Same OBJECT (like-for-like / comparable sales) and roughly same retail SCOPE, but MECHANISM differs. Side A is forward-looking guidance/outlook ("The outlook calls for like-for-like growth to accelerate in the back half") — a projected future expectation. Side B is realized past results ("Like-for-like growth in the quarter reflected traffic and ticket already realized") — an actual in-quarter outcome. Guidance about future acceleration and realized in-quarter comps are distinct causal drivers (expectation vs. realized), also spanning different timeframes (back half vs. the quarter). The names confirm it: comparable_sales_guidance vs comparable_sales. Since MECHANISM fails, default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Forward demand for next quarter ran ahead of last year.
verdicts.cited_b: Completed flights drive the amount earned this quarter.
verdicts.reason: Different OBJECT: Side A is future, not-yet-realized demand ("Forward demand for next quarter ran ahead of last year"), while Side B is realized revenue from flights already flown ("Completed flights drive the amount earned this quarter"). Different SCOPE: Side A covers next quarter's bookings; Side B covers this quarter's completed-flight revenue — different time periods and populations. Different MECHANISM: advance bookings are a forward-looking demand signal, whereas current-period passenger revenue is the earned amount from delivered service; at most correlated/downstream, not the same causal driver. All three tests fail, so DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Net sales divided by selling area gives this productivity.
verdicts.cited_b: Net sales summed across all stores gives the aggregate.
verdicts.reason: Different OBJECT: Side A is a per-unit productivity ratio (net sales DIVIDED BY selling area — "Net sales divided by selling area gives this productivity"), while Side B is an unnormalized aggregate dollar total (net sales SUMMED — "Net sales summed across all stores gives the aggregate"). A ratio (per_x="square foot") and a raw sum (per_x=null) are not the same metric even though both reference net sales. Different MECHANISM: Side A's driver is density/productivity gains as "smaller-format stores matured," whereas Side B "grew with square footage and comp gains" — i.e., adding square footage. These mechanisms are actually opposed: adding square footage raises the total but can dilute sales per square foot. Object and mechanism both fail, so the pair is DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Total sales divided by store count gives this per-store rate.
verdicts.cited_b: Net sales divided by selling area gauges productivity of the box.
verdicts.reason: Different OBJECT — the denominators differ. Side A normalizes by store count ("Total sales divided by store count gives this per-store rate."), yielding a per-store average; Side B normalizes by selling area ("Net sales divided by selling area gauges productivity of the box."), yielding a per-square-foot rate. These are distinct productivity metrics (per_x = "store" vs "square foot") that can move independently — a large-format store can have high sales-per-store yet low sales-per-square-foot. Test 1 (same object) fails, so the pair is DIFFERENT regardless of the shared auto_parts industry.

verdicts.cited_a: Comps means comparable-store sales at open stores.
verdicts.cited_b: Comps means complimentary meals given to guests.
verdicts.reason: Same label "comps" but different OBJECT: Side A defines it as "comparable-store sales at open stores" (a revenue/sales metric in auto_parts), while Side B defines it as "complimentary meals given to guests" (free food giveaways in restaurants). SCOPE differs (auto-parts retail sales vs restaurant guest freebies) and MECHANISM differs (same-store sales growth vs cost of complimentary items). This is a false-friend homonym, not the same driver.

verdicts.cited_a: Net sales are company revenue after returns and allowances.
verdicts.cited_b: Total net sales are company revenue after returns too.
verdicts.reason: All three tests hold. OBJECT: both define the metric as the same thing — company revenue after returns (net sales); A adds "and allowances," which is the standard net-sales deduction, and B's "too" explicitly mirrors A. SCOPE: both say "company" revenue, same retail population and ownership class — no segment/franchise/gross split, slice_tokens empty and per_x null on both sides; "Total" is merely an aggregate synonym for the same consolidated net sales, not a different population. MECHANISM: identical causal driver — revenue net of returns, not a downstream or correlated effect. Evidence compels sameness.

verdicts.cited_a: Adjusted operating income, which excludes costs of the store-closure program, is the measure management uses internally.
verdicts.cited_b: Operating income as reported reflects all charges recorded in the period.
verdicts.reason: Side A is a non-GAAP adjusted measure that explicitly excludes store-closure program costs ("which excludes costs of the store-closure program"), while Side B is the as-reported GAAP measure that includes everything ("reflects all charges recorded in the period"). They are the same line-item name but differ precisely by the excluded charge, so OBJECT differs (adjusted/non-GAAP vs reported/GAAP) and SCOPE differs (net of a specific item vs all-in). The rubric flags adjusted-vs-reported as a scope break, and default is DIFFERENT unless sameness is compelled. Fails the test.

verdicts.cited_a: Shoppers submitted digital carts during the promotion.
verdicts.cited_b: Recognition happens when merchandise leaves the warehouse.
verdicts.reason: All three tests fail. OBJECT differs: Side A is a count of orders placed ("Shoppers submitted digital carts"), Side B is recognized net sales revenue ("Recognition happens when merchandise leaves the warehouse") — an order-demand event vs an accounting revenue figure. SCOPE differs: online/digital shoppers during a promotion vs all merchandise shipped from the warehouse. MECHANISM differs: order placement is an upstream demand action, while revenue recognition at shipment is a downstream fulfillment/accounting trigger — at best correlated, not the same causal driver. Default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: How often diners added a drink to the order rose.
verdicts.cited_b: The rate of beverages sold alongside meals climbed.
verdicts.reason: All three tests pass. OBJECT: both name the beverage attach rate — the rate at which a drink accompanies a food order. A: "How often diners added a drink to the order rose." B: "The rate of beverages sold alongside meals climbed." ("drink" = "beverage"; "attachment" = "incidence"; "the order" = "meals"). SCOPE: identical — both restaurants, both the general diner/meal population, with no ownership-class or segment split (no company-vs-franchise, gross-vs-net distinction on either side). MECHANISM: identical causal driver — both rises are attributed to combo/bundle pricing. A: "Attachment improved with combo pricing changes." B: "Incidence rose as bundles gained traction." These are two names for one metric with one driver, so SAME.

verdicts.cited_a: Banquet and large-party commitments taken for future dates ran well ahead of the prior year despite a choppier consumer backdrop.
verdicts.cited_b: We recognize catering revenue when the event is held and the food has been delivered to the host location.
verdicts.reason: OBJECT differs: Side A is order intake -- "commitments taken for future dates" (bookings not yet fulfilled), while Side B is "catering revenue [recognized] when the event is held and the food has been delivered" (realized revenue at point of delivery). Bookings booked is not the same metric as recognized revenue. MECHANISM differs and is decisive: future commitments booked are an upstream leading indicator, and recognized revenue is the downstream realization of those same bookings once events occur. The rule forbids merging a driver with a downstream effect, so this fails object and mechanism -> DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: A percentage of operator sales flowed to us as fees.
verdicts.cited_b: Payments tied to licensee turnover reached our books.
verdicts.reason: Paraphrase of the same driver. OBJECT: both are sales-based payments to the franchisor — A "percentage of operator sales flowed to us as fees," B "payments tied to licensee turnover reached our books" (turnover = sales, licensee = operator). SCOPE: both are the franchised population paying the company (franchised operators/licensees → franchisor), same ownership class. MECHANISM: both are a payment that scales with franchisee sales (a royalty), not a merely correlated or downstream effect. The name "fee_income" does not imply an upfront one-time fee here because B's quote explicitly ties the payment to turnover, i.e., recurring sales-based royalties. All three tests hold with verbatim support → SAME.

verdicts.cited_a: Every source across the company rolls into the top line.
verdicts.cited_b: Only drinks contribute to this narrow product slice.
verdicts.reason: Different OBJECT and SCOPE: Side A is the company-wide top line ("Every source across the company rolls into the top line"), while Side B is a single narrow product category ("Only drinks contribute to this narrow product slice"). Beverage revenue is a subset/component of total revenue, not the same underlying driver — whole vs. part. Mechanism also differs (all revenue sources vs. drink sales only). Fails all three sameness tests.

verdicts.cited_a: Mix reflects what guests ordered, as premium entrees displaced value items.
verdicts.cited_b: Mix reflects the blend of premium cabin and main cabin seats sold.
verdicts.reason: Both share only the generic label "mix" but describe different OBJECTS in different industries. Side A is restaurant menu mix — "premium entrees displaced value items" (food items guests ordered). Side B is airline seat-class mix — "the blend of premium cabin and main cabin seats sold" (airfare cabin classes). Different object, different business population/scope (restaurant guests vs airline seats), and different mechanism (menu item selection vs cabin-class seat sales). Nothing compels sameness; default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Passengers, freight and other sources sum together.
verdicts.cited_b: Only hold freight contributes to this narrow amount.
verdicts.reason: Different OBJECT and SCOPE. Side A "operating_revenue" is the total: "Passengers, freight and other sources sum together" — the whole top line spanning multiple revenue streams. Side B "cargo_revenue" is a single narrow component: "Only hold freight contributes to this narrow amount." Cargo revenue is a subset of operating revenue (freight is just one of A's summed sources), so they are a part-vs-whole pair, not the same driver. Mechanism also differs: total revenue generation across all sources vs. freight-only revenue. All three tests fail; merging a total with one of its components would be permanent damage.

verdicts.cited_a: The amount a typical party spends per visit rose on menu pricing.
verdicts.cited_b: Average ticket moved higher, with pricing more than offsetting lighter add-on attach.
verdicts.reason: OBJECT (same): Both name the average spend per restaurant transaction — Side A "The amount a typical party spends per visit," Side B "Average ticket moved higher." In restaurants "average check" and "average ticket" are standard synonyms, and a party-per-visit equals one ticket/check. SCOPE (same): Both are restaurant-level with no slice tokens and no ownership/segment restriction (neither says company-owned vs franchised or segment vs consolidated), so the transaction population is symmetric. MECHANISM (same): Both increases are driven by menu pricing — A "rose on menu pricing," B "pricing more than offsetting lighter add-on attach." B only adds that add-on attach softened while pricing dominated, so pricing is the shared primary causal driver, not a downstream effect. All three tests pass, compelling SAME over the DIFFERENT default.

verdicts.cited_a: Same-store sales compare locations open in both periods.
verdicts.cited_b: Comps compare results at restaurants open in both periods.
verdicts.reason: All three criteria met with backing quotes. OBJECT: both define the same metric — sales/results at stores open in both comparison periods; "comps" is the standard restaurant-industry shorthand for comparable/same-store sales. SCOPE: identical population ("locations open in both periods" = "restaurants open in both periods"), same industry (restaurants), and neither side draws any ownership-class or segment distinction. MECHANISM: same causal driver (comparable-store sales performance), not a downstream or merely correlated effect — the two are the same measure under two names.

verdicts.cited_a: Unit sales measure the count of items sold to buyers.
verdicts.cited_b: Dollar sales measure the value of items sold to buyers.
verdicts.reason: Different OBJECT. Side A measures a count/quantity ("the count of items sold to buyers"), while Side B measures a monetary value ("the value of items sold to buyers"). Volume and dollar value are distinct metrics that move independently — units can rise while dollars fall (price cuts) or dollars can rise on flat units (price increases). Because they multiply through price rather than describe the same underlying thing, the MECHANISM also differs (volume vs. price x volume). Criterion 1 fails, so the pair is DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Orders placed through the app and website climbed.
verdicts.cited_b: Purchases made through our digital ordering channels rose.
verdicts.reason: Same OBJECT: both describe digital-channel sales at a restaurant — "the app and website" (A) and "our digital ordering channels" (B) refer to the identical thing, since a restaurant's app and website ARE its digital ordering channels; "orders placed" and "purchases made" both denote digital sales. Same SCOPE: both cover the total digital channel with no distinguishing sub-population — no company-owned vs franchised, no segment vs consolidated, no gross vs net split on either side. Same MECHANISM: A says orders "climbed" and B says purchases "rose" — both are the same upward driver (growth in digital ordering volume), not a correlated or downstream effect. All three criteria met with verbatim support, so the compelling-evidence bar for SAME is cleared.

verdicts.cited_a: Warehouses we own and staff handle our flow.
verdicts.cited_b: Outsourced providers fulfill on a contract basis.
verdicts.reason: Near-opposite ownership classes, so all three sameness tests fail. OBJECT: Side A is owned physical warehouses ("Warehouses we own and staff"), Side B is external contracted providers ("Outsourced providers fulfill on a contract basis") — different things. SCOPE: company-owned/insourced vs third-party/outsourced — exactly the ownership-class split that forces DIFFERENT. MECHANISM: internal owned facilities handling flow vs contracted external fulfillment are distinct causal drivers, not the same one. Fails all three; default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Average spend rose to 19.40 dollars per guest.
verdicts.cited_b: Per-guest receipts averaged about 19.40 dollars.
verdicts.reason: All three tests pass. OBJECT: both describe the mean amount one diner pays per visit — Side A "Average spend rose to 19.40 dollars per guest" and Side B "Per-guest receipts averaged about 19.40 dollars" report the identical metric at the identical value ($19.40), and "average check" and "average spend per guest" are industry-standard synonyms in restaurants (total receipts / patrons served). SCOPE: both are per-guest, restaurants industry, with no distinguishing ownership class (company vs franchised) or segment vs consolidated split on either side — same population. MECHANISM: both attribute the move to the per-visit average payment edging up, not a downstream or merely correlated effect. The exact-matching dollar figure and per-guest framing compel sameness.

verdicts.cited_a: Typical annual takings at one location edged up.
verdicts.cited_b: Mean yearly sales per unit improved over the period.
verdicts.reason: OBJECT: Both name the same metric — average annual sales per single restaurant location. "auv" is the standard industry abbreviation for Average Unit Volume, which is precisely "average restaurant sales volume." A: "Typical annual takings at one location" equals B: "Mean yearly sales per unit" (unit = one restaurant). SCOPE: Both are per single location in the restaurants industry (A per_x "restaurant"; B "per unit"). Neither restricts to company-owned vs franchised or segment vs consolidated, so they sit at the same population level with no conflicting qualifier. MECHANISM: Both describe the same causal driver — per-location annual sales rising ("edged up" / "improved over the period"), not a downstream or merely correlated effect. All three tests hold, so the default-DIFFERENT is overcome.

verdicts.cited_a: Pricier beef and dairy lifted the cost of ingredients.
verdicts.cited_b: Restaurant-level profitability narrowed as a result.
verdicts.reason: Side A is an input-cost cause (rising beef/dairy ingredient costs); Side B is the profitability outcome that follows from it. The OBJECT differs: A describes the cost of ingredients, B describes restaurant-level margin/profitability — a cost input versus a profit result. The MECHANISM also differs: B's own quote says profitability narrowed "as a result," explicitly marking it as the downstream effect of A, not the same causal driver. The rule forbids merging a cause with its downstream effect. Fails OBJECT and MECHANISM, so DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Fuel spend divided by gallons gives this per-gallon price.
verdicts.cited_b: Fuel spend summed for the period gives the total expense.
verdicts.reason: Fails OBJECT: Side A is a per-gallon unit price (a rate, dollars per gallon) — "Fuel spend divided by gallons gives this per-gallon price" — while Side B is an aggregate dollar total for the period — "Fuel spend summed for the period gives the total expense." A ratio and a summed total are different things. Fails SCOPE: A measures per gallon (per_x=gallon); B is a period-wide total (per_x=null). Fails MECHANISM: per-gallon price is only ONE input to total expense — Side B's own quote says "Consumption and price together set the expense line," so the per-gallon price is a component/upstream factor of total expense, not the same driver. Related but not identical → DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Buyers held items to collect later at the counter.
verdicts.cited_b: Checkout scanning marks the point of recognition.
verdicts.reason: OBJECT differs: Side A is items reserved/held for later pickup ("Buyers held items to collect later at the counter.") — a pending, not-yet-completed hold. Side B is sales recognized at the register ("Checkout scanning marks the point of recognition.") — a completed, rung-up sale. MECHANISM differs: reserving/holding an item for future pickup is a pre-sale set-aside, whereas checkout scanning is the point-of-sale recognition trigger; a held order is at best a downstream-or-upstream correlate of a rung-up sale, not the same causal driver. Even though both share the auto_parts industry and an in-store setting, the underlying thing and its recognition mechanism are not the same, so at least two of the three tests fail. Default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: The count of restaurants, net of closures, expanded.
verdicts.cited_b: Locations added net of those we retired increased the footprint.
verdicts.reason: All three tests pass. OBJECT: both describe the net count of restaurant units — A's "count of restaurants, net of closures" equals B's "Locations added net of those we retired... footprint." SCOPE: both are the total restaurant/location population in the restaurants industry with no ownership-class or segment distinction (neither specifies company-owned vs franchised, neither is a subsegment). MECHANISM: both are the same causal driver — net unit expansion, i.e. gross new units minus units closed/retired ("net of closures" = "net of those we retired"; "expanded" = "increased the footprint"). No differing dimension, so this is the same underlying driver.

verdicts.cited_a: Profitability at units we own and run directly.
verdicts.cited_b: Royalty income net of operator support programs.
verdicts.reason: All three tests fail. OBJECT differs: Side A is operating "Profitability at units we own and run directly" (a profit margin on store operations), while Side B is "Royalty income net of operator support programs" (a fee-based revenue stream). SCOPE differs on ownership class — the textbook company-operated vs franchised split: Side A covers company-owned units ("units we own and run directly"), Side B covers franchised units (royalty income from franchisees, net of "operator support programs"). MECHANISM differs: earning margin by running owned stores is not the same causal driver as collecting royalties from franchise operators. Any one failure forces DIFFERENT; here all three fail.

verdicts.cited_a: Time from leaving the gate to reaching the next added up.
verdicts.cited_b: The full taxi-and-fly duration per trip was tallied.
verdicts.reason: All three tests pass. OBJECT: both describe the same metric — total gate-to-gate elapsed time of a flight. "Block hours" is the airline-industry term for block time (off-blocks to on-blocks), i.e. from leaving one gate to reaching the next; A says exactly that, and B's "full taxi-and-fly duration" (taxi-out + fly + taxi-in) is precisely that same gate-to-gate span. SCOPE: both airlines, both aggregate totals of that duration ("added up" / "per trip was tallied"), with no differing ownership class, segment, or population. MECHANISM: both measure the same actual aircraft operating time from departure gate to arrival gate — the same causal driver, not a correlated or downstream effect. Block hours and gate-to-gate time are the identical underlying driver.

verdicts.cited_a: Diners held tables through our platform before arriving.
verdicts.cited_b: The register captures amounts at the moment guests pay.
verdicts.reason: OBJECT differs: Side A is a count of reserved tables ("Diners held tables through our platform before arriving.") while Side B is recorded sales dollars ("The register captures amounts at the moment guests pay.") — a booking event vs. a monetary amount. MECHANISM differs: a pre-arrival reservation is an upstream intent that may or may not convert (no-shows, walk-ins pay without booking), whereas a register-recorded sale is a downstream point-of-payment event. SCOPE also differs (people who booked ahead vs. all paying dine-in guests). At least one of the three tests fails, so default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Management now expects full-year sales toward the high end of the prior range.
verdicts.cited_b: Sales recognized in the quarter reflected completed customer transactions.
verdicts.reason: All three tests fail. SCOPE: Side A is full-year ("full-year sales"); Side B is the quarter ("recognized in the quarter") — different periods. MECHANISM: Side A's driver is management raising its outlook (a projection); Side B's driver is revenue recognition from completed transactions (an accounting fact) — a forecast is not the same causal driver as booked sales. Defaulting to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Our like-for-like sales at stores open more than a year rose modestly.
verdicts.cited_b: Sales at outlets operating for more than twelve months improved on stronger do-it-yourself demand.
verdicts.reason: OBJECT (same): A's "like-for-like sales at stores open more than a year" and B's "Sales at outlets operating for more than twelve months" are synonyms for the identical retail metric — comparable store sales with the same >12-month definition. SCOPE (same): both cover existing stores in the auto_parts industry at the consolidated level, with no ownership-class distinction (company-owned vs franchised) or segment split on either side. MECHANISM (same): both describe existing-store sales growth as the driver; B's "on stronger do-it-yourself demand" is a sub-reason explaining WHY the same comp-sales figure rose, not a different or downstream driver. All three tests pass with verbatim support, so the two are the same underlying driver.

verdicts.cited_a: Services segment revenue reflects memberships and repair.
verdicts.cited_b: Total revenue reflects products and services combined.
verdicts.reason: Different OBJECT and SCOPE. Side A is the Services segment only ("Services segment revenue reflects memberships and repair") — a subset driven by services (memberships/repair). Side B is the consolidated total ("Total revenue reflects products and services combined") — the whole company including products. A segment is a proper subset of total revenue, not the same underlying metric; scope differs (segment vs consolidated) and mechanism differs (services-only vs products+services). Fails at least two of the three tests, so DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Members accumulated rewards from their recent visits.
verdicts.cited_b: Expired unredeemed balances flow to the income line.
verdicts.reason: These are opposite ends of the loyalty-program lifecycle, not the same driver. OBJECT differs: Side A is rewards being earned/accrued ("Members accumulated rewards from their recent visits"), which builds a deferred liability; Side B is expired, never-redeemed balances recognized as revenue ("Expired unredeemed balances flow to the income line"). MECHANISM differs and is in fact inverse: Side A is driven by member visits/spending creating an obligation (issuance), whereas Side B is driven by non-redemption/expiration releasing that obligation into income (breakage). A rise in points issued does not produce breakage income; breakage arises from points NOT being used. Same industry and loyalty population is not enough — object and mechanism both fail, so DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Total revenue includes company sales and franchise royalties.
verdicts.cited_b: Total revenues include company sales and franchise royalties.
verdicts.reason: All three tests pass. OBJECT: both describe total revenue as the same metric. SCOPE: both define it identically as the consolidated aggregate spanning the same two populations/ownership classes — company-owned ("company sales") plus franchised ("franchise royalties"). MECHANISM: both are the same causal driver, the sum of those exact two components, not a downstream or merely correlated effect. The only differences are trivial grammar (singular "revenue"/"includes" vs plural "revenues"/"include"); the evidence compels sameness.

verdicts.cited_a: Adjusted pre-tax income excludes special mark-to-market items.
verdicts.cited_b: Pre-tax income includes those special mark-to-market items.

verdicts.reason: Same OBJECT: occupancy expense is definitionally the cost of occupying store space, i.e. rent + utilities + related location charges — so A's "cost of keeping store space open" and B's "Rent, utilities and related charges for our locations" name the identical underlying cost item. Same SCOPE: both refer to the company's own retail store locations ("store space" / "our locations"), consolidated, with no franchise-vs-owned or segment distinction. Same MECHANISM: both are the direct cost of occupying physical store space as the driver, not a downstream or merely correlated effect. All three tests hold with verbatim support, so the evidence compels sameness.

verdicts.cited_a: The federally posted dairy benchmark climbed through the period.
verdicts.cited_b: Spot cheddar traded on the exchange set what we pay for slices.
verdicts.reason: Different OBJECT: Side A is a "federally posted dairy benchmark" (Class III milk price — a regulated milk price), while Side B is "Spot cheddar traded on the exchange" (a market cheese block price). Milk price and cheese price are distinct metrics, not the same thing. Different MECHANISM: the exchange-traded spot cheddar is an upstream commodity input, whereas the federal milk benchmark is a separately-posted, formula-derived price; they are correlated within the dairy complex but not one causal driver. Same SCOPE (both restaurants) is insufficient. OBJECT and MECHANISM fail, so per the default-to-DIFFERENT rule, these are different drivers.

verdicts.cited_a: Profit as a share of revenue widened over the year.
verdicts.cited_b: Operating profit per dollar of revenue improved on cost discipline.
verdicts.reason: OBJECT: same metric — operating margin and return on sales are synonyms for operating profit / revenue. A's name operating_margin pins its generic "profit as a share of revenue" to operating profit, matching B's explicit "operating profit per dollar of revenue." SCOPE: same — both restaurants, both slice_tokens empty, per_x null; no company-owned vs franchised or segment vs consolidated split; both are whole-company margins. MECHANISM: same — both describe margin expansion (widened/improved); the "cost discipline" in B is the reason the same margin driver moved, not a different or downstream driver. All three tests hold.

verdicts.cited_a: System-wide sales include franchised and company sales.
verdicts.cited_b: Company sales include only the company-operated sales.
verdicts.reason: Different OBJECT and SCOPE. Side A "system-wide sales include franchised and company sales" is a superset spanning both franchised and company-owned populations; Side B "company sales include only the company-operated sales" is a narrower subset restricted to the company-operated ownership class, explicitly excluding franchised. This is the franchised-vs-company-owned ownership-class split that forces DIFFERENT. Mechanism also differs (total-system aggregate vs company-owned revenue line). Defaulting to DIFFERENT is correct.

verdicts.cited_a: Shipping bottlenecks delayed inbound merchandise flow.
verdicts.cited_b: Empty shelves rose across several important categories.
verdicts.reason: Different OBJECT and MECHANISM. Side A ("Shipping bottlenecks delayed inbound merchandise flow.") is an upstream logistics cause — the inbound freight/port pipeline. Side B ("Empty shelves rose across several important categories.") is a downstream outcome — shelf out-of-stock levels. These sit in a cause-and-effect chain, not the same driver: empty shelves can arise from many causes besides freight delays (demand spikes, labor shortages, ordering errors). Sameness rule forbids merging a driver with its downstream/correlated effect, so DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Mainline revenue reflects flying performed by our own aircraft and crews.
verdicts.cited_b: Consolidated results also fold in capacity purchased from regional partners under fixed-fee arrangements.
verdicts.reason: Different SCOPE and OBJECT. Side A's mainline revenue covers only "flying performed by our own aircraft and crews" — a strict subset. Side B's consolidated revenue additionally "fold[s] in capacity purchased from regional partners under fixed-fee arrangements," so it spans a broader business population (mainline + regional) and a different revenue aggregate. Mainline is a component of consolidated, not the same measure; merging a subset into its superset would be over-merging. Fails scope and object tests, so DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Prepaid installs not yet performed remain a liability.
verdicts.cited_b: Completing the work moves amounts to earned income.
verdicts.reason: Different OBJECT and MECHANISM. Side A is a balance-sheet liability: "Prepaid installs not yet performed remain a liability" — cash collected before the service is delivered, held as unearned/deferred revenue. Side B is income-statement recognized revenue: "Completing the work moves amounts to earned income" — the point at which performance is complete and the amount becomes earned. These are two opposite ends of the same revenue-recognition lifecycle: A is the unperformed liability, B is the downstream recognition event when work is finished. B is the effect of discharging A, not the same underlying thing. A liability balance and an earned-revenue recognition are distinct objects driven by distinct mechanisms. Fails object and mechanism tests -> DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Deep discounts were used to move slow-selling inventory.
verdicts.cited_b: The reported profitability rate slipped year over year.
verdicts.reason: Fails OBJECT and MECHANISM. Side A is a promotional pricing action (discounting inventory to clear stock) — a driver/cause. Side B is a financial outcome metric (gross margin / profitability rate declining) — a downstream effect. Heavy discounting is only ONE possible cause of margin decline (cost inflation, mix shift, or shrink could equally explain it), so the two are at best causally correlated, not the same underlying driver. Per the rule, a downstream effect must not be merged with its cause. DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: The share of flights arriving within schedule improved despite weather.
verdicts.cited_b: Arrival reliability strengthened on better turn execution at hubs.
verdicts.reason: Same airlines industry and same network-operations scope, but the evidence does not compel sameness. OBJECT is unclear/not identical: A measures "share of flights arriving within schedule" — a punctuality (on-time) rate — while B cites "arrival reliability," a broader reliability notion that need not equal on-time share. MECHANISM diverges: A's gain came "despite weather" (a headwind overcome, no operational lever named), whereas B's came "on better turn execution at hubs" (a specific hub-turnaround lever) — different causal stories. With OBJECT unclear and MECHANISM differing, the default-DIFFERENT / "if unclear → DIFFERENT" rule applies; over-splitting is cheap and reversible, over-merging is not.

verdicts.cited_a: Capacity measures available seat miles across the fleet.
verdicts.cited_b: Capacity measures throughput across the distribution fleet.
verdicts.reason: Same label "capacity" but different drivers on all three tests. OBJECT: A is available seat miles (aircraft flight capacity, per_x "available seat mile"); B is throughput across a distribution fleet (goods moved through logistics, per_x null) — different underlying things. SCOPE: A is the airlines industry, B is retail — different business populations, and "fleet" means aircraft on A vs distribution vehicles on B. MECHANISM: flying-capacity supply vs distribution-network throughput are distinct causal drivers. All three fail, so DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Stage length gives the average miles flown per trip.
verdicts.cited_b: Dwell time gives the average minutes a party occupies its table.
verdicts.reason: Fails all three tests. OBJECT differs: Side A measures "average miles flown per trip" (a distance) while Side B measures "average minutes a party occupies its table" (a duration) — different metrics entirely. SCOPE differs: airlines vs restaurants — different industries and business populations. MECHANISM differs: flight distance per trip vs table occupancy time are unrelated causal drivers. Nothing compels sameness; default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: European sourcing shifts with the single-currency cross.
verdicts.cited_b: Overall import buying power follows a trade-weighted basket.
verdicts.reason: OBJECT differs: Side A is the euro/dollar bilateral rate ("the single-currency cross"), while Side B is a multi-currency broad index ("a trade-weighted basket") — the euro is only one component of that basket, not the basket itself. SCOPE differs: Side A is limited to "European sourcing," whereas Side B covers "Overall import buying power" across all trade partners. MECHANISM differs: a single-currency cross and a trade-weighted index are correlated but distinct causal drivers that can move in opposite directions. All three tests fail, so DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Passenger revenue divided by travelers gives this per-head rate.
verdicts.cited_b: Fare revenue divided by miles flown by paying travelers gives another.
verdicts.reason: Different OBJECT and MECHANISM. Side A's denominator is the number of travelers, yielding a per-head rate ("divided by travelers gives this per-head rate"). Side B's denominator is passenger-miles ("divided by miles flown by paying travelers"), yielding a per-mile figure — the standard airline "passenger yield." Because one normalizes by passengers and the other by passenger-miles (distance), they are distinct metrics driven by distinct mechanisms: total fare per person vs. fare intensity per mile. A route can score high on one and low on the other, so they are not the same underlying driver. Default to DIFFERENT holds.

verdicts.cited_a: Total revenue divided by members gives this per-member rate.
verdicts.cited_b: Total revenue summed across the company gives the aggregate.
verdicts.reason: Different OBJECT: A is a per-member ratio ("per-member rate") while B is a raw aggregate ("the aggregate") — a normalized rate is not the same thing as a raw total. Different SCOPE: A is normalized per member (per_x "member"), B is company-wide with no denominator (per_x null). Different MECHANISM: A's value changes with BOTH revenue and member count, whereas B changes only with total revenue; A is a downstream derivation (B divided by members), not the same underlying causal driver. All three tests fail, so DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Long-dated government paper reflects distant expectations.
verdicts.cited_b: Short maturities track near-term policy bets instead.
verdicts.reason: Different OBJECT and SCOPE: Side A is the 10-year Treasury yield ("Long-dated government paper"), Side B is the 2-year Treasury yield ("Short maturities") — distinct points on the yield curve. Different MECHANISM: A reflects "distant expectations" (long-term growth/inflation), while B tracks "near-term policy bets" (Fed rate path). These are separate, well-known drivers (the 2s10s spread depends on them differing).

verdicts.cited_a: Adjusted unit cost excludes fuel and special charges.
verdicts.cited_b: Reported unit cost includes fuel and special charges.
verdicts.reason: Both are unit cost per available seat mile in airlines, but the evidence compels DIFFERENT on all three tests. SCOPE fails decisively: Side A "excludes fuel and special charges" while Side B "includes fuel and special charges" — a gross-vs-net measurement-basis difference, and fuel is typically an airline's largest cost component, so the two measures can diverge materially. OBJECT differs: adjusted vs reported are distinct constructed metrics measuring different cost bases. MECHANISM differs: reported unit cost is driven in part by fuel-price volatility, whereas adjusted unit cost is deliberately built to strip fuel out and isolate non-fuel operating efficiency. Contradictory quotes (excludes vs includes) confirm these are different causes; default-to-DIFFERENT stands.

verdicts.cited_a: Upfront amounts spread across the multi-year agreement.
verdicts.cited_b: Ongoing income accrues as operator sales occur.
verdicts.reason: Different OBJECT and MECHANISM. Side A is the upfront/initial franchise fee recognized over time ("Upfront amounts spread across the multi-year agreement") — a time-based amortization of a one-time payment. Side B is ongoing royalty revenue driven by franchisee sales volume ("Ongoing income accrues as operator sales occur") — a sales-based accrual. These are two distinct franchise revenue streams with distinct causal drivers (contract term elapsing vs. operator sales occurring). Same industry/franchise scope does not rescue sameness when object and mechanism diverge. Default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Gross franchise revenue includes ad-fund amounts collected.
verdicts.cited_b: Net franchise revenue excludes ad-fund amounts collected.
verdicts.reason: SCOPE test fails, which is decisive. Side A is GROSS franchise revenue ("includes ad-fund amounts collected"); Side B is NET franchise revenue ("excludes ad-fund amounts collected"). Gross vs net is an explicit distinguishing class in the rubric, and here the two measures differ by exactly the ad-fund component. Though the broad category (franchise revenue, restaurants) overlaps, the OBJECT differs (gross-with-ad-fund vs net-without-ad-fund) and the measured population is not identical. A merge would conflate two different revenue measures. Default to DIFFERENT holds.

verdicts.cited_a: Load factor gives the share of seats sold on flights.
verdicts.cited_b: Utilization gives the share of hours each aircraft flies.
verdicts.reason: Different OBJECT and MECHANISM. Side A's load factor measures "the share of seats sold on flights" — a passenger demand/capacity-fill metric (how full the planes are). Side B's utilization measures "the share of hours each aircraft flies" — an asset-productivity metric (how intensively the fleet is used). Seats-sold and hours-flown are distinct things driven by distinct causes: a plane can fly many hours (high utilization) yet carry few passengers (low load factor), and vice versa. Object, scope (passenger seats vs aircraft hours), and mechanism all fail, so DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Gross sales are total sales before returns are removed.
verdicts.cited_b: Net sales are total sales after returns are removed.
verdicts.reason: Different OBJECT and MECHANISM. Side A is "total sales before returns are removed" (gross); Side B is "total sales after returns are removed" (net). These are distinct metrics differing by exactly the returns adjustment, and the rubric explicitly names gross vs net as different measurement classes. Net sales is a downstream derivative of gross sales (gross minus returns), not the same causal driver.

verdicts.cited_a: System-wide sales include company and franchised locations.
verdicts.cited_b: Systemwide sales include company and franchised locations.
verdicts.reason: Same OBJECT: both name the same metric (A: "System-wide sales", B: "Systemwide sales") — only the hyphenation differs, a spelling variant of the identical restaurant-industry term. Same SCOPE: A's "include company and franchised locations" and B's "include company and franchised locations" describe the identical business population and ownership mix (company-owned plus franchised). Same MECHANISM: both are the aggregate sales across all owned and franchised locations, the same causal driver, not a downstream or merely correlated effect. All three tests pass with verbatim quotes on each side.

verdicts.cited_a: Adjusted operating income excludes restructuring charges.
verdicts.cited_b: Operating income includes those restructuring charges.
verdicts.reason: These are the adjusted vs. GAAP versions of operating income, not the same driver. Side A explicitly EXCLUDES restructuring charges ("Adjusted operating income excludes restructuring charges") while Side B explicitly INCLUDES them ("Operating income includes those restructuring charges"). SCOPE fails: adjusted-vs-reported is a different measurement basis, the same class of distinction as gross-vs-net that the rubric flags. MECHANISM fails: the restructuring charges are precisely what separates the two, and they even move in opposite directions (adjusted rose 12% vs. reported declined 3%), proving the adjustment is material and the two are not the same underlying cause. Over-merging an adjusted metric with its GAAP counterpart would erase the very charge that distinguishes them, so default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Prepaid program dues amortize across the coverage term.
verdicts.cited_b: Ratable recognition posts each month of the plan.
verdicts.reason: Side A ("Prepaid program dues amortize across the coverage term.") is the deferred/unearned membership-fee liability being drawn down on the balance sheet. Side B ("Ratable recognition posts each month of the plan.") is the recognized membership-fee income on the income statement. OBJECT differs: an unearned deferred liability is not the same thing as earned revenue. MECHANISM differs too — recognizing the income is the downstream EFFECT of amortizing the deferred balance, i.e. a cause→effect chain rather than the same causal driver. Two of the three criteria fail, so default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Every unit, company and operator alike, is included.
verdicts.cited_b: Only locations we run ourselves make this tally.
verdicts.reason: Same OBJECT (restaurant count) but the SCOPE differs by business population and ownership class. Side A is system-wide: "Every unit, company and operator alike, is included." Side B is restricted to company-owned only: "Only locations we run ourselves make this tally." System count (company + operator) is a strictly larger population than company-only count, so these are different drivers. Fails the SCOPE test -> DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Gross merchandise value counts all marketplace orders.
verdicts.cited_b: Net revenue counts only the commission we retain.
verdicts.reason: Fails all three sameness tests. OBJECT differs: A is total value of all marketplace orders ("Gross merchandise value counts all marketplace orders" / "1.2 billion dollars across all sellers") while B is only the commission retained ("Net revenue counts only the commission we retain" / "180 million dollars of commission as our net share"). SCOPE differs: A is gross across all sellers, B is the company's net commission share (gross vs net ownership class). MECHANISM differs: net commission revenue is a downstream slice of GMV, merely correlated, not the same causal driver. Classic gross-vs-net trap → DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Regional segment cost reflects purchased affiliate flying.
verdicts.cited_b: Consolidated operating cost reflects mainline and regional.
verdicts.reason: Fails all three tests. OBJECT differs: A is a single segment's cost, B is a company-wide operating total. SCOPE differs: A covers the regional segment only ("Regional segment cost ... purchased affiliate flying"), while B is consolidated across both mainline and regional ("Consolidated operating cost reflects mainline and regional") — A is a subset of B, not the same population. MECHANISM differs: A is driven specifically by purchased affiliate (regional) flying, whereas B aggregates mainline plus regional. A segment-level regional cost is not the same underlying driver as the consolidated cost that contains it, so default DIFFERENT holds.

verdicts.cited_a: Prepayments on special orders await the arrival of parts.
verdicts.cited_b: Delivery of the ordered item books the transaction.
verdicts.reason: Different OBJECT and MECHANISM. Side A describes customer prepayments/deposits collected in advance and held while awaiting parts (an unearned liability before any sale). Side B describes revenue recognition triggered at delivery (a booked sale). These are opposite ends of the transaction lifecycle, not the same underlying driver. Fails the same-object and same-mechanism tests, so default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Gross passenger revenue is stated before taxes collected.
verdicts.cited_b: Net passenger revenue is stated after taxes remitted.
verdicts.reason: OBJECT overlaps (both describe passenger revenue), but SCOPE fails: Side A is "Gross passenger revenue...stated before taxes collected" while Side B is "Net passenger revenue...stated after taxes remitted." Gross-vs-net is an explicit scope mismatch (before-tax vs after-tax measurement of the same population). The two figures differ by the tax layer, so they are different measures, not one shared driver. Per the default-to-DIFFERENT rule and the gross-vs-net scope test, this fails the SAME criteria.

verdicts.cited_a: Adjusted SG&A excludes transformation and severance costs.
verdicts.cited_b: SG&A expense includes transformation and severance costs.
verdicts.reason: Fails the same-OBJECT test. Side A is a non-GAAP adjusted measure that explicitly EXCLUDES transformation and severance costs; Side B is the reported SG&A expense that explicitly INCLUDES those same costs. The two differ by exactly the transformation/severance items, so they are distinct measures (adjusted vs reported), not the same underlying driver. Default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Block hours measure time from gate push to gate arrival.
verdicts.cited_b: Flight hours measure time from wheels up to wheels down.
verdicts.reason: Different OBJECT and MECHANISM. Block hours measure gate-to-gate time (from pushback at the departure gate to arrival at the destination gate), which includes taxi and ground time. Flight hours measure only airborne time ("wheels up to wheels down"), excluding all ground/taxi time. These are two distinct aviation duration metrics measuring different spans of an operation, so they are not the same underlying driver.

verdicts.cited_a: What we spent to promote the brand through media rose.
verdicts.cited_b: Outlays to reach shoppers with campaigns increased.
verdicts.reason: OBJECT differs: Side A is advertising specifically "through media" (paid media buys), while Side B is broader "campaigns" to "reach shoppers" — marketing spend. Advertising is a subset of marketing (which also covers direct mail, in-store, loyalty, email, non-media outreach), so they are not the same underlying object. SCOPE matches (both retail, no ownership/population split) and MECHANISM is broadly similar (promotional outlays), but a similar mechanism cannot rescue a differing object. Per the default-to-DIFFERENT rule and the subset relationship, these are different drivers.

verdicts.cited_a: Aircraft we fly ourselves carry this per-seat figure.
verdicts.cited_b: Capacity purchased from affiliates carries another.
verdicts.reason: Both express a unit cost per available seat mile (same OBJECT type), but SCOPE and MECHANISM differ. Side A covers mainline, company-operated flying ("Aircraft we fly ourselves carry this per-seat figure."), while Side B covers regional capacity bought from separate affiliate carriers ("Capacity purchased from affiliates carries another."). That is a different business population and ownership class — the airline analog of company-owned vs franchised — and the cost is driven by a different mechanism (own operating costs vs capacity-purchase agreements with affiliates). Scope and mechanism fail, so per the default this is DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Growth at stores open a full year measured demand.
verdicts.cited_b: Like-for-like locations showed how underlying demand moved.
verdicts.reason: OBJECT: both describe comparable/same-store sales — "stores open a full year" (A) and "like-for-like locations" (B) are the same metric, and "comps" is the standard shorthand for "comparable_store_sales_growth." SCOPE: identical store population (established/like-for-like store base), same auto_parts industry, with no differing ownership class (neither splits company-owned vs franchised). MECHANISM: both use it as a gauge of underlying demand — "measured demand" (A) and "showed how underlying demand moved" (B). All three tests pass with verbatim support, so the compelling-sameness bar overcomes the DIFFERENT default.

verdicts.cited_a: Locations staffed and run by our own teams anchor the fleet.
verdicts.cited_b: Shops operated inside partner retailers under license agreements expanded.
verdicts.reason: Different OWNERSHIP CLASS and SCOPE: Side A is company-operated ("staffed and run by our own teams"), while Side B is licensed/third-party-operated ("operated inside partner retailers under license agreements"). This is the classic company-owned vs franchised/licensed split, which the rubric treats as different scope. OBJECT also differs (own-team fleet vs licensed partner shops) and MECHANISM differs (anchoring the existing fleet vs licensed-location expansion). Any one failure forces DIFFERENT; here all three fail.

verdicts.cited_a: Restaurant sales divided by units gives this per-store rate.
verdicts.cited_b: Restaurant sales summed across units gives the total.
verdicts.reason: Different OBJECT and SCOPE. Side A is a per-store rate: "Restaurant sales divided by units gives this per-store rate" (per_x: restaurant) — a ratio/average isolating single-store productivity. Side B is an aggregate total: "Restaurant sales summed across units gives the total" (per_x: null) — the summed consolidated revenue across all units. One is divided by units (per-unit average), the other is summed across units (total); they are mathematically related (Total = AUV × unit count) but distinct drivers, since total sales also captures unit-count growth that AUV deliberately strips out. MECHANISM differs too: AUV measures per-store demand/density, total sales measures overall revenue scale. Fails same-object and same-scope tests, so DIFFERENT.
